message.content:
The year begins to cough  
in the attic of the house.

Pipes shudder.  
The calendar curls at the edges  
like paper held too close  
to a quiet, invisible flame.

In the pantry,  
the flour has learned a new word:  
mold  
that blooms between Thursdays  
and no one remembers  
leaving the bag open.

Outside, the hinges of the door  
forget  
how winter is supposed to sound.  
January steps in  
wearing a thin raincoat,  
apologizing for not bringing  
its usual frost.

Thermostats lose track of loyalty.  
They waver,  
confused generals  
consulting forecasts that lie  
with a confident smile.  
Air conditioners hum in months  
that once belonged to sweaters  
folded like small, obedient clouds  
in the closet.

The neighborhood map softens.  
Puddles appear  
where there were never puddles,  
ferns colonize the cracks  
in concrete that never asked  
for anything green.

The insects  
rewrite their own attendance sheets.  
Mosquitoes arrive early,  
sign in twice,  
stay late.  
Ticks push the fence posts  
northward on the atlas  
while no one is looking.

The old dog on the porch  
pants in the wrong season.  
Her fur blows out  
in a hot wind  
that came from no one’s childhood.

In the hospital,  
asthma visits more often,  
a relative with no respect  
for visiting hours.  
Children learn to say  
“particulate”  
before they can spell  
“butterfly.”

The river under the bridge  
is a nervous employee,  
sent home early,  
then ordered back in  
with impossible deadlines—  
flood this street,  
recede by Monday,  
don’t disturb the tourists.

In the grocery store,  
oranges carry price tags  
like medical bills.  
The clerk shrugs:  
storms in the place  
where the blossoms should have been.  
On the radio, a voice explains  
how coffee is forgetting  
the altitude of its birthplaces.

Some mornings,  
the horizon looks pixelated,  
as if the sky were a video  
buffering  
on a weak connection.  
Birds arrive late to their own songs,  
their sheet music  
smudged by a season  
that skipped rehearsals.

Insurance adjusters  
speak a new dialect—  
deductibles measured  
in once-a-century events  
now scheduled  
like annual reviews:  
See you next year  
after the water,  
after the wind.

A farmer stands in a field  
that doesn’t remember  
being a field.  
The soil is a tired archive  
of unfiled weather reports.  
He holds seeds in his fists  
like old family photos,  
watched by clouds  
that wander past  
without signing their names.

Across town,  
the city’s power grid  
starts to stutter,  
a fluorescent throat clearing.  
Blackouts flicker through the evening  
like someone testing  
the universe’s light switch.  
Elevators pause mid-floor,  
the building’s nervous system  
forgetting which way is up.

In the classroom,  
a teacher unrolls a world map  
that is already outdated.  
The blue parts  
are rumors.  
The green parts  
are promises made  
by people who have never  
held a dying tree.

Students practice new careers:  
resilience officer,  
retreat planner  
(for coastlines),  
historian of snow.

The house plants  
by the window  
tilt their leaves  
as if listening  
to conversations in the street  
about evacuation routes,  
zoning laws,  
the best time to sell.

At night,  
the city breathes headlights,  
sleeps in a blanket of hum.  
Somewhere in the ceiling of the sky,  
ventilation fails.  
The whole atmosphere  
is a room  
with the windows painted shut,  
the thermostat broken,  
everyone arguing  
over who left the oven on  
while the wallpaper  
warps in slow motion.

This is not  
a malfunction that resets  
when you flip the switch.  
This is the house  
we built over three centuries,  
the wiring braided  
with exhaust and profit,  
the foundation sunk  
in drilled and fractured stone.

The blueprints are still on the table.  
They have always been  
on the table.  
Our signatures multiply  
on every page.

Downstairs, in the fuse box,  
numbers climb and blink red.  
The meter spins  
like a siren without sound.  

We are not  
trapped guests.

We are the landlords  
of this overheating room,  
standing in the doorway  
with every tool we need  
hanging,  
within reach,  
on the wall.
